summary: Drew DeVault outlines his philosophy for writing effective release notes for open source software. He argues release notes should be clerical documents prioritizing breaking changes and upgrade steps, not marketing material. The detailed changelog should follow a brief summary of flagship improvements, ideally generated from git shortlog. He strongly advocates for annotated git tags as the only appropriate place for release notes, rejecting both CHANGELOG files and GitHub releases. He shares a custom tool called 'semver' to automate the process and provides a real-world example from wlroots.
key_points: Release notes should lead with breaking changes and required upgrade steps, not exciting new features
The detailed changelog should be comprehensive so users can check if specific bugs were fixed
Annotated git tags are the only appropriate place for release notes — not CHANGELOG files or GitHub releases
Good git discipline and git shortlog can automate most of the changelog generation
Plaintext format naturally prevents over-embellishment with images, videos, or marketing fluff
money_quotes: Note that this mostly applies to free and open source software, which is the only kind of software which is valid.
When I read the release notes, the first thing I need answered is: "what do I need to do when I upgrade?"
Keep it short — remember that we're not advertising the release, but facilitating the user's upgrade. This is a clerical document.
In my opinion, there's only one appropriate place for it: an annotated git tag.
I don't like "CHANGELOG" files and I definitely don't like GitHub releases.
It's also written in plaintext, which conveniently prevents you from being too extra with your release notes — no images or videos or such.
key_insight: Release notes are clerical upgrade documents, not marketing — lead with breaking changes, use annotated git tags, and let git shortlog do the heavy lifting.
